text:
Quick sketch: Oliver Lin — runtime SWE candidate (loop 2)

Context / meta:
- Interviewer: Jordan Blake (notes here), loop included 1x system-design, 1x coding, 1x behavioral + team match.
- Role we’re hiring for: Senior Runtime Engineer (inference-focused) — emphasis: batching, kv cache, graceful degradation.
- Timeboxed: 60m system design, 40m algo/code. Candidate comfortable with C++/Rust and some Go.

High-level impression (gut):
- Smart, pragmatic. Good instinct for production tradeoffs (latency vs cost).
- Less polished on low-level memory accounting and some concurrency corner cases.
- Great at framing tradeoffs and owner mentality — would be a good match for a product-forward runtime team if paired with a strong SRE for correctness/borrowing discipline.

Signals — strengths:
- Prefix/KV cache intuition: gave clear examples of when to do per-request vs shared caches, how to inva...

text:
Purpose:
This is a working doc capturing results from the autoscale policy simulator runs we executed after the Feb 26 dedicated pool degradation, plus a draft isolation checklist for noisy-neighbor-like behavior observed during the incident. Goal: produce a compact runbook patch we can ship into on-call playbooks and iterate from there.

Context / brief incident summary (Feb 26 chain):
- 2026-02-26 03:17 UTC: first alert — elevated GPU queuing latency on dedicated-gpu-pool-3; tracer showed continuous token service latencies rising for 30+ mins.
- 03:22 UTC: autoscaler attempted scale-up but did not acquire spare nodes due to mis-evaluated capacity (policy threshold incorrectly used pod_cpu_request instead of pod_gpu_request).
- 03:40 UTC: noisy tenant (svc-internal-batch) started heavy memory allocations causing localized OOM + driver resets on 2/16 GPUs.
- 04:05 UTC: manual eviction...

title: auth rate sleuth logbook
preview:
Context:
- Sporadic 401s and 429s observed from mobile app (iOS release channel) calling /v1/generate and /v1/chat/completions between 2026-03-02 and 2026-03-09. Desktop and server-to-server traffic largely unaffected.
- Users report transient failures ~1-3s into a session or immediately after app background/foregro...
text:
Context:
- Sporadic 401s and 429s observed from mobile app (iOS release channel) calling /v1/generate and /v1/chat/completions between 2026-03-02 and 2026-03-09. Desktop and server-to-server traffic largely unaffected.
- Users report transient failures ~1-3s into a session or immediately after app background/foreground.
- Hypothesis: short-lived client key + network retries causing quota bursts, or a bug in auth cache eviction.

Raw traces / snippets (redacted keys and ids):
- Example curl that reproduces locally (simulated):
 curl -v https://api.redwood.ai/v1/generate 
 -H "Authorization: Bearer <REDACTED_KEY>" 
 -H "Content-Type: application/json" 
 -d '{"model":"redwood-small","input":"say hi"}'

- 401 response body seen in mobile logs:
 2026-03-05T18:21:12.482Z request_id=3a9f8b2a status=401 path=/v1/generate src=mobile-ios-1
 response: {"error":"invalid_api_key","message":"API ke...

text:
Notes to self - ad hoc breadcrumbs for the throughput regression that popped up after the prefetch tuning work. Mostly raw commands, error snippets, and hypotheses. Keep this as an index for a more formal postmortem later.

Summary (quick):
- Regression observed 2025-11-05 after changing continuous prefetch to larger window to improve latency p50 for short requests. Instead saw sustained throughput drop under mixed sequence lengths during peak traffic.
- Symptoms: requests per second dropped ~3.5x under typical traffic profile; GPU utilization appeared high but token output stalled (low tokens/sec).
- Initial suspicion: prefetch window + batching interaction -> head-of-line blocking & KV cache thrash. Also possible: eager KV loads saturate PCIe/CPU memory copy path causing stalls.

Timeline / quick log of experiments:
- 2025-11-05 09:20 local reproduce: ramped synthetic load with seq_...

text:
Working draft that became the source for the final writeup.

Incident date: 2026-02-14
Severity: P0

What happened
- EU (eu-central) ran out of schedulable GPUs in the hosted pool. Router kept accepting traffic and queued requests; tail latencies blew up and then 503s increased.

Impact
- Hosted API in EU: p99 > 25s for ~23 minutes.
- Some customers saw 503s; failover to us-east did not trigger for all tenants because of a policy exception.

Root cause
- Capacity planner thresholds were tuned for average, not burst. A batch of long-context workloads arrived after a model launch email.
- Autoscaler max was capped too low for eu-central due to a stale quota assumption.

Follow-up action items (count these)
1) Raise eu-central autoscaler max and keep a 15% warm buffer for long-context models. Owner team: Eng SRE.
2) Add routing policy that forces cross-region failover when queue depth > ...

text:
Working doc not final

Goal
- Provide a quick internal timeline + a consistent set of phrases for customer comms around the rollback loop / stuck rollback issue in Redwood Private upgrades (RRB-17).

Preferred terminology
- Use: upgrade rollback stuck or rollback loop
- Avoid: infinite loop (sounds scary), data loss (not observed)

Timeline (PT)
- Feb 11: Eng sees repeated rollback retries in lab; workaround identified (delete rollback lock CM + restart controller).
- Feb 12: Customer reports (Acme, Northwind). Incident opened (INC-2147). INT-330 created for patch tracking.
- Feb 13: Root cause confirmed (lock not cleared when cleanup hook exits). PR drafted for fix + loop detection.
- Feb 14: Customer escalation call; validated workaround sequencing + guardrails.
- Feb 16: Draft customer-safe guidance circulated for review.

title: KV eviction stochastic simulator results (WIP)
preview:
Purpose and quick summary:

This doc captures the results from a small stochastic simulator I (Jordan) put together to explore how randomized eviction decisions interact with real-world reuse patterns and memory bandwidth peaks. The goal was not to build the final policy, but to get intuition about: 1) how conservat...
text:
Purpose and quick summary:

This doc captures the results from a small stochastic simulator I (Jordan) put together to explore how randomized eviction decisions interact with real-world reuse patterns and memory bandwidth peaks. The goal was not to build the final policy, but to get intuition about: 1) how conservative vs aggressive eviction thresholds affect long tail latency and paging, 2) what eviction noise (randomized kicks) does to reuse curves, and 3) how these behaviors map to expected PCIe/GPU memory bandwidth bursts.

Context and motivation
- We've been seeing intermittent latency spikes on <seqlen>1000</seqlen> workloads when KV occupancy approaches 85-95% (see JIRA-RT-1187). On those spikes we observe heavy DMA bursts followed by elevated page faults. Hard to reproduce in lab because of multi-tenant noise.
- Hypothesis: slightly suboptimal eviction heuristics can cause evi...

text:
Context:
- Late-stage dev: small perf/regression spike observed in hosted text-generation v2 route (low-prompt-length).
- Customer report + internal smoke tests: occasional incoherent completions (~0.6% -> 1.9% of sample). Need fast triage before escalation.

Objective (5–20 minute prioritization):
- Rapidly determine if regression is model, routing, or pre/post-processing.
- Produce small reproducible sample set + ad-hoc rubric to categorize failure mode.

Quick hypothesis list (ordered cheap->expensive):
1) Prompt tokenization change (tokenizer update or subtle encoding mismatch).
2) Quantization/precision path change (new kernel selection for short sequences).
3) Routing fallback to smaller model variant under burst.
4) Prompt truncation or prefix drop due to cache bug.
5) Data corruption in the request body at edge (rare but check).

Ad-hoc rubric (for quick labeling):
- Category ...

title: Meta Instruction Orchestrator - seam proto jotter
preview:
Context and motivation:

- Goal: lightweight runtime shim that sits between clie...
text:
Context and motivation:
- Quick working notes for an "meta" instruction orchestrator (MIO): an adapter layer that accepts high-level, possibly multi-turn directives and composes them into executable sub-instructions (prompts, tool calls, model variant routing).
- Goal: lightweight runtime shim that sits between client SDK and core runtime; aims to encode composition rules, safety boundaries, and observability hooks at the instruction orchestration level rather than inside each app.
- Target use-cases: multi-step transforms (summarize+translate+extract), user-facing automation flows (generate code + run tests), and tenant-level policies (cost budgets, model pinning).

Non-goals (for this proto):
- Not trying to be a full workflow engine (no long-running state machines).
- Not implementing secure plugin sandboxing here — will assume tool calls are proxied through existing plugin runtime...
